items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: In sonata form, in what key does the second subject normally appear in the exposition of a piece in a major key?
items.why: It's Dominant. “Tonic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer is credited with significantly expanding the coda in sonata-form movements?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Beethoven. “Mozart” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main sections does classical sonata form contain (exposition, development, recapitulation)?
items.why: The real figure is 3 sections. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
